sections.heading: RT
sections.content:
External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) is a useful modality for select patients with locoregional or metastatic neuroendocrine or adrenal tumors. Decisions to use EBRT should be made in a multidisciplinary manner, considering patient factors, disease site/stage, and other therapeutic options available [NE-I 1 of 2].

Principles:
1. EBRT may be considered for any histologic subtype of NETs (including well-differentiated G1/2/3, NECs, MiNENs, PCCs, and PGLs) [NE-I 1 of 2]
2. Site-specific principles used for other primary cancer types are generally applicable [NE-I 1 of 2]
3. Higher EBRT doses have been associated with higher rates of disease control, but may be associated with increased risk of adverse events [3], [NE-I 1 of 2]
4. IMRT/VMAT techniques with IGRT may be needed to maintain precision and reduce toxicity [NE-I 1 of 2]
5. When administering SBRT/SABR, IGRT techniques and motion management are strongly recommended [NE-I 1 of 2]
6. EBRT generally does not play a role in locoregional NETs of small bowel, appendix, or colon (including mesenteric disease) due to risk of bowel injury. However, focal EBRT may be considered in select circumstances [NE-I 1 of 2]
7. EBRT may be considered in locoregional NETs of lung, thymus, and GI tract locations where resection may have significant morbidity [NE-I 1 of 2]

Dose Frameworks:
- Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT)/Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR): total dose varies by site and organ tolerance, high dose per fraction, ≤5 fractions, daily or every other day. Indication: Oligometastatic disease at multiple sites including liver, adrenal, bone, lung, mediastinum, head and neck, and lymph nodes; liver-limited metastases with <5 lesions [NE-I 1 of 2]
- Hypofractionated RT: total dose varies, moderate dose per fraction, 6–20 fractions, daily. Indication: Most frequently used regimen for functional NETs to improve hormonal symptoms [NE-I 1 of 2]
- Conventional fractionation with concurrent chemotherapy: total dose varies by site, standard dose per fraction, fractions vary, daily with concurrent radiosensitizing chemotherapy. Indication: Locoregional unresectable disease, particularly for atypical carcinoid histology or tumors with higher Ki-67 [NET-11]

Approaches:
- Palliative RT for symptomatic bone metastases: dose fractionation Hypofractionated (≤10 fractions) preferred, no concurrent chemotherapy. Indication: Painful bone metastases, impending pathologic fracture, or cord compression [NE-I 1 of 2]. Toxicities: Fatigue, local pain, possible vertebral compression fracture.
- Liver-directed EBRT (SBRT/SABR): dose fractionation ≤5 fractions, ablative dose, no concurrent chemotherapy. Indication: Limited (<5) liver metastases. May be used alone or in combination with other liver-directed therapies [NE-I 1 of 2]. Toxicities: Radiation-induced liver disease, fatigue, nausea.
- RT ± concurrent fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy for locally advanced unresectable disease: dose fractionation varies, concurrent chemotherapy fluoropyrimidine-based (eg, capecitabine). Indication: Locally advanced unresectable disease in GI NETs and PanNETs (excluding small bowel mesenteric); locally advanced unresectable lung/thymic NETs [NET-10, NET-11, PanNET-13]. Toxicities: Site-specific toxicities, chemotherapy-related toxicity.
- RT ± concurrent Cisplatin/Etoposide or Carboplatin/Etoposide: dose fractionation standard fractionation, concurrent chemotherapy Cisplatin/Etoposide or Carboplatin/Etoposide. Indication: Locoregional unresectable intermediate-grade (atypical carcinoid) lung/thymic NETs. Thought to have greatest efficacy in tumors with atypical histology or higher Ki-67 [NET-11]. Toxicities: Esophagitis, pneumonitis, myelosuppression.
- Definitive chemoradiation for NEC: dose fractionation standard fractionation, concurrent chemotherapy Cisplatin + etoposide or carboplatin + etoposide. Indication: Resectable extrapulmonary poorly differentiated NECs when definitive chemoradiation is selected instead of surgery [PDNEC-1]. Toxicities: Myelosuppression, mucositis, esophagitis, nausea/vomiting.

timeline.title: EBRT Regimens
timeline.body: The input describes multiple RT regimens: SBRT/SABR (≤5 fractions), hypofractionated RT (6–20 fractions), and conventional fractionation with concurrent chemotherapy. Approaches include palliative bone RT (≤10 fractions), liver-directed SBRT (≤5 fractions), and chemoradiation for unresectable disease or NEC. No explicit total dose in Gy was provided; typical doses vary by site.
